content.problemStatement:
In Chefland, the speed of light is c m/sc\ \mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}c m/s, and acceleration due to gravity is g m/s2g\ \mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}^2g m/s2.

Find the smallest height (in meters) from which Chef should jump such that during his journey down only under the effect of gravity and independent of any air resistance, he achieves the speed of light and verifies Einstein's theory of special relativity.

Assume he jumps at zero velocity and at any time, his velocity (vvv) and depth of descent (HHH) are related as
v2=2⋅g⋅H.v^2 = 2 \cdot g \cdot H.v2=2⋅g⋅H.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two integers g and c separated by a space.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum height H (in meters) required for Chef to reach the speed of light. H = c^2 / (2·g). The answer is guaranteed to be an integer.

solution.approach: For each test case we directly apply the kinematic formula v^2 = 2·g·H. Setting v = c gives H = c^2 / (2·g). Because the input guarantees that 2·g divides c^2, the division yields an integer. We compute the numerator c*c using 64‑bit integer (or BigInt) to avoid overflow, divide by (2*g) and output the result. The algorithm is O(1) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each of the T test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are stored regardless of T.

code.C:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){int T; if(scanf("%d",&T)!=1) return 0; while(T--){ long long g,c; scanf("%lld %lld",&g,&c); long long ans = c*c/(2*g); printf("%lld\n",ans); } return 0; }
code.Python3:
import sys

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        g = int(next(it))
        c = int(next(it))
        out.append(str(c*c//(2*g)))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
